channel.title: Comments for page "Mr. Parker"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "Mr. Parker"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sweet Jesus I hope he survived and got out of 1983 alive

Did he?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good story, I thought it was very interesting. I have always wondered if Any D-Class manage to live through their time at the Foundation and what happened to them. But I have two questions, 1. How did he survive SCP-1983?
2. Did he have his mind wiped when he left? Cause I'm not too sure based on the conversation alone

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Show opinion post
- Hide click HERE to close

I do like the monologuing, “Talks With Mann” style structure, and connection to the heart house. Shedding more light on the D-Class’s life outside of the Foundation.

Like Kalinin said, it doesn’t ‘develop’ anything appreciably further with this specific D-Class (who is known to be heroic from a previously established SCP), but I can appreciate it for what it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm. I don't care for the lampshading of profligate D-Class use in the narrator's dialogue. It doesn't make a whole lot of sense, either, as a sales pitch. "Hey, you don't know this, but we used to use you as a human guinea pig and send you into life-threatening situations because we didn't think of you as human. Anyway, hey, we're better now, so it's cool right? Want to come do it again? You'll have to respond to a number again, though."

Also, and don't take this the wrong way, but there's sort of an uncomfortable racial element to a white authority figure (I'm assuming he's white because he says to the guy "hey you look like the black guy from Ghostbusters) finding a working-class black guy in Detroit and asking him if he wants to be used in human experimentation. "We'll pay you handsomely in the end" doesn't strike me as particularly compelling either, given, you know, history.

More than anything, though, despite its intentions this piece does nothing to develop the character of the heroic D-Class from SCP-1983. What it does is develop the character of the guy sent to retrieve said D-Class. If that was your intention, then I suppose that's okay, but given the comments in here I don't think that's the case. Everything about the D-Class character is given to us through the eyes of the narrator. There is no opportunity for the D-Class to do anything (except apparently agree to coming back after a five-minute conversation) or say anything. So the vantage point is from the Foundation's end, and frankly the operative here seems so painfully unaware of the context of his actions and his request that I can't really take this very seriously.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You took a difficult premise(fleshing out something from a very popular scp) and did it very well. There's a lot of personality here. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"This summer, only one man can save the Foundation. Ernie Hudson is… D-14134"

Its a pretty interesting premise to have the Foundation go on to recruit a former D-class (which is a unique enough title in and of itself) to come back and assist with yet more anomalous shenanigans. One that I can get behind.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is amazing, touching, and quite heroic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to everyone who took a look at this in chat, apologies to the admin who saw that I accidentally uploaded a version of this too early for the contest, and a big thanks to everyone who worked on the fantastic SCP-1983, which obviously inspired this tale a great deal.

Your feedback is greatly appreciated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting, you have taken one of the most well-known D-class characters and provided context to his life. It would seem unusual for a former D-class to know what he had been through and willing to go back there… but this does imply subtle differences in how the D-class system works here.
